figures.caption: Figure 6: GDPO-normalization ablation on Qwen2.5-1.5B with the full multi-reward + KL-Cov (βcov=0.05) objective. Replacing per-channel z-score with a single combine-then-normalize step degrades GSM8K substantially and MATH500 / MMLU-Pro consistently across the entire trajectory; the gap widens past step 160 (vertical line). Stars mark the overall peak per benchmark.
Table 1: Performance on three math-reasoning benchmarks and two code-reasoning after extended training (18 epochs). Best unsupervised value per column in bold.
Figure 5: INTUITOR + KL-Cov ablation on Qwen2.5-1.5B (steps 0–280). KL-Cov (βcov=0.02) doubles step-160 accuracy versus vanilla INTUITOR but does not prevent collapse past step 200.

Table 5: KL-Cov sweep merged across the INTUITOR (single-signal) and multi-reward base methods on Qwen2.5-1.5B, reporting peak / step-160 pass@1 (%). The peak captures best-case accuracy; the step-160 column reflects mid-horizon stability under continued training. Within each base reward (top: INTUITOR sweep; bottom: multi-reward sweep), KL-Cov is the only variable. Bold marks the chosen full-method configuration. Top block: KL-Cov on single-signal INTUITOR roughly doubles step-160 retention on math benchmarks (e.g., GSM8K 22.4 → 46.2%) but cannot keep pace with the complementary reward structure of multi-reward, and on a longer horizon (≥ 200 steps) eventually collapses too. Bottom block: KL-Cov is remarkably insensitive to βcov—a 200× range produces only ∼2.7 pp of step-160 spread.

Figure 2: Accuracy (pass@1) trajectories on GSM8K, MATH500, MMLU-Pro for Qwen2.5-1.5B (top) and Qwen2.5-3B (bottom). The dashed vertical line marks step 160 (Table 1); the star (⋆) marks the peak among unsupervised methods (GRPO-GT excluded), labeled in-axis. Only multireward + KL-Cov tracks GRPO-GT through the full 340-step horizon at both scales.

Figure 3: Single- & multi-reward analysis (no KL-Cov) on Qwen2.5-1.5B. Left: GSM8K and MATH500 pass@1. Right: training-time mean completion length and mean entropy. Cluster-only (blue) shows answer-mode collapse (rising entropy, lengthening generations); INTUITOR (green) shows token-mode collapse (entropy in [0.03, 0.17], length crashing to ∼100 tokens); multi-reward without KL-Cov (red) blocks both exploits but eventually collapses by step 260.

Figure 1: Given a prompt, we sample G completions from the rollout policy and compute two intrinsic rewards: an answer-level cluster-voting reward over final-answer agreement, and a completion-level self-certainty reward over token-level predictive distributions. Each reward is normalized within the prompt group before being combined into rollout-level advantages. Optimization uses a GRPO-style objective, while KL-Cov penalizes only high-covariance tokens to regularize concentrated updates.

Table 6: Training, optimization, and evaluation hyperparameters used across all configurations. Values are shared between Qwen2.5-1.5B and Qwen2.5-3B unless explicitly listed per-model. The format-reward weight applies only to the multi-reward configurations that include the boxed-format auxiliary; KL-Cov entries apply only when KL-Cov is on.
Table 2: Pass@1 (%, greedy) on AIME 2024 and AIME 2025 at the step-160 checkpoint (18 epochs). Best unsupervised value per column in bold (ties bolded together).

Figure 4: KL-Cov βcov sweep on the full multi-reward objective (Qwen2.5-1.5B). All three coefficients (βcov ∈ {0.0005, 0.05, 0.1}) maintain stable accuracy and completion length through step 280; the no-KL-Cov baseline (red) collapses around step 240.

Figure 7: Logprob-space gradient norms per reward channel under each normalization variant. Left: cluster+format channel norm. Under combine-then-normalize (red) the cf channel is roughly an order of magnitude weaker than under per-channel GDPO (purple); the channel is being effectively silenced by the naïve combination. Right: self-certainty channel norm. Combine-then-normalize is 10–20× larger, i.e. the higher-variance channel dominates the policy update by precisely the ratio of pre-normalization standard deviations. Both panels use a logarithmic y-axis. Vertical dashed line at step 160 matches Figure 6.
